segments.s632-g41: به جایی is a locative/ directional phrase ('to/at a place'); که introduces a relative clause ('that/which'); باشد is the classical present/subjunctive copula here meaning 'is' (used in subordinate clauses); همیشه‌بهار is a compound adjective 'ever-spring/evergreen' describing that place. Word order is noun + relative clause (place — that is ever-spring).
segments.s632-g42: نسیم بهار literally 'breeze (of) spring' (the ezafe is omitted in poetry), functioning as the subject; آید is the present/optative/poetic form 'comes'; از جویبار is a source phrase 'from the brook/stream' indicating where the breeze comes from.
segments.s632-g43: This is a simple nominal list with the copula: گهر هست 'there is a gem' (noun before the copula, a common poetic order), followed by و دینار و گنج درم — a coordinated list 'and dinars and treasure (in the purse)'. درم functions as 'money/dirham' here, completing the inventory of wealth.
segments.s632-g44: چو is a temporal/conditional 'when/if'; باشد is the present/subjunctive copula 'there is'; درم again 'money/dirham' as the thing whose existence is asserted; دل نباشد is a negated clause 'the heart is not' (نباشد = negative of 'باشد'); به غم literally 'to/with sorrow' = 'in sorrow'. The clause means 'when there is money, the heart is not in sorrow.'
segments.s632-g45: نوازنده شاهی is noun + adjective: 'a royal/kingly musician' (شاهی as an adjective). که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; از مهر تو is a causal/ablative phrase 'from (his) love for you' — مهر = love/affection and تو = you, indicating the motive for his laughter.
segments.s632-g46: بخندد is the present/poetic 3rd-person form 'he laughs'; چو = 'when/whenever'; بیند is 'he sees' (present); همی is a habitual particle meaning 'always/regularly' (here strengthening the temporal sense); چهر تو = 'your face'. The clause reads 'who, out of your love, laughs whenever he sees your face.' Note the verb-first poetic ordering.
segments.s632-g47: به سالی is a temporal phrase 'in a year' or 'per year'; دو بارست is a poetic fused form meaning 'is twice' (دو بار است → 'it is two times'); بار درخت 'the tree's time/fruiting' — together the phrase says that the tree has two fruitings/occasions in a year. Poetic word order places the temporal phrase first.
segments.s632-g48: ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from'; قنوج is the place-name Kannauj; برنگذرد is the negative present/future sense 'will not pass/go away (beyond)'; نیک‌بخت is the subject 'the fortunate one' placed after the verb for emphasis — so 'the fortunate one will not pass from Kannauj' (i.e. the lucky man does not leave that place).
segments.s632-g57: 'ز بهرام' uses the preposition ز (poetic for از) to mark the person from whom the question is asked (i.e., 'asked of Bahram'). 'زان پس' is a temporal sequence ('after that' / 'thereupon'); بپرسید is a past tense 3rd-person singular verb 'he asked'; نام is the direct object 'name' and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s632-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause ('for/that'); بی‌نام is an adjective 'nameless' describing the kind of reply; پاسخ is the noun 'answer'; نبودی is a past-tense copula/verb (here negated) meaning 'would not be / was not' in classical narrative style; تمام functions as an adjective 'complete/whole' describing the reply. Together the clause explains the reason why he asked the name.
segments.s632-g59: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; بشنید is past tense 3rd-person 'he heard'; the proper name بهرام is the subject (poetically following the verb); رنگ رخش is a noun phrase 'the color/appearance of his steed' (رنگ + possessive noun رخش). The phrase as a whole: 'When Bahram heard (it) — the color/appearance of his horse...'.
segments.s632-g60: دگر شد is a fixed poetic expression 'became different/changed' (literally 'became other') describing a change of aspect or countenance. که تا begins the following subordinate clause giving the content or consequence; تا here works like 'so that / as to' (introducing purpose or scope) and چون is interrogative 'how'; دهد is present/subjunctive 3rd-sing 'he may give / should give' used for reported indirect speech; پاسخش is 'his answer' with the enclitic -ش. Overall: 'his look changed, (so that) he wondered how he should give his answer.'
segments.s632-g61: به فرجام is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at the end / finally'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (here the lemma list gives noun senses, but context is the verb); ای is the vocative particle 'O'; سخن‌گوی مرد is a vocative nominal phrase 'speaker/eloquent man' (literally 'speaker of men' or 'eloquent man'). The whole unit is the speech-introductory clause 'at last he said, "O eloquent man,..."'.
segments.s632-g62: مرا + را here are split in the token list but combine to form the object 'مرا' ('me'); در دو کشور is a prepositional phrase 'in both/ the two realms' (در = in, دو کشور = two countries/realms). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'; روی زرد is the object phrase 'a pale face' or 'make (my) face pale' (روی = face; زرد = pale). Grammatically this is an imperative request: 'Do not make my face pale in the two realms.'
segments.s632-g63: من is the first-person pronoun 'I'; از شاه ایران literally 'from the King of Iran' marks the source/agent from whom something might be asked; نپیجم is a negative first-person form meaning 'I will not beg / I will not ask' (negative prefix ن- plus verb, used here as a principled refusal); به گنج is the goal/object 'for treasure' (به + گنج). The clause states refusal: 'I will not ask the King of Iran for treasure.'
segments.s632-g64: گر is the conditional 'if'; از نیستی is a poetic expression 'from nonexistence' (i.e., even if I am nothing); چند is an indefinite 'some/ a little' emphasizing small worth or quantity; باشم is the 1st-person subjunctive of 'to be' used in the protasis 'if I be / if I am'; به رنج means 'in/with hardship' (be in suffering). The clause offers a stoic resolution: 'If I am but little or nothing, let me be in hardship.'
segments.s632-g73: کجا is an interrogative adverb placed at the clause front ('where'). آن بزرگان خسرونژاد is a noun phrase: آن is a demonstrative ('those/that'), بزرگان is the plural noun 'great ones', and خسرونژاد is a post‑nominal compound indicating lineage ('of the Khosrow line'); Persian allows such attributive compounds after the head. The clause contains no verb (elliptical rhetorical question), typical of epic style.
segments.s632-g74: جهاندار is a title/epithet 'sovereign' or 'world‑holder' that heads this appositive phrase; it directly attaches to the proper names that follow. The two names are coordinated by و ('and'), so the whole phrase is in apposition to آن بزرگان خسرونژاد. There is no copula — Persian often stacks titles and names without a linking verb.
segments.s632-g75: دگر آنک is 'that other one' (دگر = 'other', آنک a demonstrative/relative 'that which/that one'); دانی تو is 'you know' with the verb دانی (2nd‑person present) and the explicit subject تو for emphasis. بهرام را is the marked person (را is the object/topic marker); word order here is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (subject pronoun placed before the verb and the marked object after it).
segments.s632-g76: This is a string of epithets in apposition to بهرام: جهاندار (title), پیروز (adjective 'victorious'), خودکام (adjective 'autocratic'). The final را is an emphatic/topic marker attaching to the whole descriptive phrase (a poetic duplication of the object marker), highlighting the named figure.
segments.s632-g77: اگر introduces a conditional. من is the explicit first‑person subject (added for emphasis). ز (poetic form of از) + فرمان او means 'from his command' (i.e., 'if I go beyond / disregard his command'). بگذرم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I would pass/stray' used in the protasis of a hypothetical conditional.
segments.s632-g78: جهان is the subject ('the world') and سرآرد (3rd‑person present/future) means 'will bring/raise/bring about' — poetic inversion places the verb after. به مردی is an indirect/objective phrase 'a certain man' introduced by به, and بر سرم is a prepositional phrase 'upon my head' with the possessive enclitic -م; together the clause says the world will set/raise a man upon the speaker.
segments.s632-g79: نماند is a negated present meaning 'will not remain' (used prophetically/factually in narrative). بر و بوم is a fixed collocation 'land and home' (بر and بوم together denote territory), and هندوستان is the genitive specifying which land. The verb preceding the noun phrase is a poetic inversion common in epic lines.
segments.s632-g80: به ایران is a directional prepositional phrase 'into/to Iran'. کشد is the verb meaning 'will draw/carry' (poetic present/future); خاک جادوستان is the object 'the soil of the sorcerers' (خاک 'soil' + جادوستان genitive 'of the magicians/sorcerers'). The literal meaning: the soil (land) of the sorcerers will be carried to Iran — a metaphorical way to say the realm will be taken.
segments.s632-g89: زِ پاسخ: the preposition ز (here 'from' / 'on/at [the hearing of]') marks cause — 'on/at the reply'. پر آژنگ شد: پر = 'full (of)'; آژنگ is an archaic/poetic adjective (see note) and شد is the past of شدن 'became' → 'became full of آژنگ'. روی شاه: 'روی' = 'face' and 'شاه' = 'the king'; the possessor follows the noun here (the king's face). Word order shows the causal phrase first, then the predication ‘became full…’, with the possessed noun (روی شاه) after the verb in this poetic inversion.
segments.s632-g90: چنین گفت: 'thus said' — گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (even if the provided lemma senses were nominal, here it functions as verb) and چنین = 'thus/so'. اگر دور ماند ز راه: a conditional clause introduced by اگر 'if'; دور ماند is an older/poetic conditional sense where ماند (lit. 'remains') is used to mean 'should remain/if he remains' (optative/conditional sense rather than simple present), and ز راه = 'from the road' (i.e. 'away from the road'). The whole clause is what the king utters.
segments.s632-g91: یکی چاره سازم کنون من که روز: یکی = 'one'/'a certain' used to mean 'a (single) remedy' or 'some plan'; چاره سازم = the verb سازم (1st-person singular) with چاره → 'I devise/shall make a remedy' (in classical Persian present often has future/intentive force: 'I will devise'); کنون من = 'now I' (temporal emphasis). که introduces the subordinate/purpose clause begun by روز in the next segment ('that a day...').
segments.s632-g92: سرآید بدین مرد لشکر فروز: سرآید is a classical present/optative meaning 'may come' / 'will arrive' (here purpose/expectation: 'a day shall come'). بدین is written here as two tokens (ب + دین) in the gloss, but should be read as به + این = 'to/for this' (i.e. 'for this man'); مرد = 'man' (here 'this man', likely a hero) is the beneficiary/target, and لشکر فروز = 'army + فروز', where فروز is an adjectival/epithet 'bright/glorious' placed after the noun in poetic word order → 'his glorious army' or 'an army made glorious'. The clause thus means 'a day will come to/in which this man's army will be made glorious.'
segments.s632-g65: "جزین" (جز این) opens with an exclusive sense 'except/none but this' and is followed by the jussive/copula "باشد" meaning 'may it be'. "آرایش دین ما" is the noun phrase 'the ornament/adornment of our religion' (دین = religion, ما = our) serving as the predicate that "باشد" applies to.
segments.s632-g66: "همان" (= 'that same') modifies the following nominal phrase. "گردش" here means 'course/turn/circuit' (a poetic word for 'course'), and "راه و آیین ما" = 'our way and our custom/practice' with the possessive "ما" placed after the coordinated nouns. The whole phrase spells out what the ornament (آرایش) consists of.
segments.s632-g67: "هرانکس که" = 'whoever (that)'. The verb "پیچد" (present/subjunctive form) here means 'turns' (turn away), and the phrase "سر از شاه خویش" = 'his head from his king' (سر + از marks turning away), with "خویش" realized as خوی + enclitic ش = 'his own'. The clause describes someone who abandons or looks away from his sovereign.
segments.s632-g68: "به برخاستن" uses به + infinitive/masdar to mean 'on/when rising; upon departing'. "گم کند" is a present/future-oriented clause where کند is the ordinary present 'does/makes' and گم is 'lost' (so 'causes to be lost' / 'will lose'). "راه خویش" = 'his way/path' with the possessive enclitic. The verb is placed at the end, typical of classical Persian order.
segments.s632-g69: "فزونی نجست" literally 'sought not increase' — نجست is the past-root 'جست' with negative (did not seek). "آنک بودش خرد" is a relative clause 'who/that had wisdom', where "بودش" is the past copula with enclitic (it/for him it was) meaning 'he had'. Thus: 'He who had wisdom did not desire more.'
segments.s632-g70: "بد و نیک" = 'bad and good'. "بر ما همی بگذرد" means 'pass over us' with بر marking 'over/onto' and همی + present verb (بگذرد) giving a continuous/habitual sense ('still/continually passes'). The verb sits at the end, as is typical in older Persian.
segments.s632-g71: "خداوند گیتی فریدون" is an appositive nominal phrase: 'Fereydun, lord/sovereign of the world' (خداوند here = 'lord/sovereign' as an honorific). "کجاست" is the interrogative 'where is' (کجا + ست 'is').
segments.s632-g72: "که" introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. "پشت زمانه" literally 'the back/support of the time/age' means 'the support/protector of the era'; "بدو بود راست" = 'was true for/to him' (بود = 'was' in past, راست = 'true/right/firm'). The clause says he served as a firm support for his age.
segments.s632-g1: ز is a poetic preposition meaning 'because of' and introduces the cause phrase (ز بهرام = 'because of Bahram'). شنگل is treated here as the subject (likely a proper name or obscure noun). شد (past of شدن) is the light verb 'became' and takes اندرگمان as the resulting state ('became in doubt/uneasy'). The whole phrase literally: 'Because of Bahram, Shangal became in doubt.'
segments.s632-g2: که introduces the content of Shangal's doubt ('that ...'). این repeated before فر and برز is demonstrative emphasis ('this farr and this exaltation'). و is the simple coordinating conjunction linking nouns; تیر و کمان is a conventional pair 'arrows and bow(s)'. The clause is an incomplete list introduced by that/conjunction, left to be completed in following lines.
segments.s632-g3: نماند is the past negative/negative-result sense of ماندن 'remain/ be left' (here 'was left/there remained not'). همی is a poetic/emphatic particle (here reinforcing the assertion). این فرستاده را places 'this messenger' with the object/topic marker را after the noun, marking it as the item affected by the verb. Word order (verb before noun phrase) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s632-g4: The correlative negatives نه ... نه ... enumerate what is lacking: 'neither Indian nor Turk nor noble.' Each adjective/noun is listed, and the final را functions as an emphatic/topic marker attaching to the whole coordinated phrase. This is the standard 'neither ... nor ...' construction.
segments.s632-g5: اگر begins a conditional ('if'). خویش 'by his nature/disposition' (here 'by nature'). شاهست‌گر is a compressed/poetic form meaning 'if he is a king/kingly' (شاه + است + -گر/گر used in old/poetic Persian to mark conditional or alternative). مهترست is the second alternative 'or if he is the greater/chief' — the line presents two possible grounds (kingly nature or seniority).
segments.s632-g6: برادرش خوانم is a main clause where خوانم (1st person singular) means 'I call' (call/name him) and the pronominal suffix ش on برادر marks 'his brother' — 'I call him his brother' (idiomatically: 'I call him my/brother'). هم is an adverb 'also/too'. اندر is the preposition 'in/among', and خورست is a predicate meaning 'is at the table/among the eaters' (here describing presence at the feast). Note the compact poetic word order: object/pronoun suffixes and verb forms convey person and relations.
segments.s632-g7: بخندید is a perfect/past-tense verbal form 'he/they laughed'. و simply links the two actions. بهرام را places Bahram as the indirect object/topic (marked by را) of the subsequent گفت. گفت here is the past-tense 'said' (poetic word order places گفت before its subject شاه, so the speaker 'the king' follows the verb).
segments.s632-g8: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic vocative/introduction to direct speech — the 'که' here leads into the address and the -ای gives the vocative force ('O ...'). پرهنر is a vocative epithet 'skilled/cultured one'. با is the preposition 'with' or 'bringing', governing گهر 'the gem'. پیشگاه means 'in the presence of the court/throne' — the phrase 'با گهر پیشگاه' is a compact imperative idea in poetry: 'with the gem, before the presence' (i.e. 'present the gem before the court'). The imperative verb is implied by the address and omitted for brevity.
segments.s632-g9: This is a fronted noun phrase containing a vocative/address and an emphasized object: برادر 'brother' is the noun, توی شاه را places شاه (the king) in a direct-address/relational position (here functioning like “O king” or “your king”) and is followed by the object-marking را. بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly’ qualifying what follows. Word order is emphatic/poetic: the phrase is set up before the rest of the clause and the object marker را signals its syntactic role even though the main verb comes later.
segments.s632-g10: بدین here functions as a prepositional/adverbial 'with these' (classical form written as ب + دین = 'بدین'), introducing a list of items. بخشش is used in the sense of 'gift/grant/tribute' and is followed by a coordination of زور (strength/might), تیر (arrow) and کمان (bow). The particles و simply join the coordinate nouns; the whole phrase supplies the instruments/attributes of the person introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s632-g11: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'for'); فر کیان is a genitive compound meaning 'the royal glory (فر) of the Kian dynasty (کیان)'. داری is 2nd-person singular present 'you have' (or 'he has' depending on the antecedent—classical Persian allows some flexibility in subject reference), and و زور شیر coordinates a second nominal attribute 'and the might/strength of a lion' (زور = strength, شیر = lion).
segments.s632-g12: نباشی is a negative present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (here expressing a hypothetical/conditional meaning: 'you would not be'), followed by مگر 'except/unless' introducing the exception. نامداری is a noun 'renown/notability' and دلیر is its qualifying adjective 'brave'. Together the phrase means 'you would not be (so) except by having the renown of a brave (man)'.
segments.s632-g13: بدو is a dative/adverbial pronoun 'to him' (indicating the indirect object of گفت). گفت is the past tense 'said' (classical narrative past), and بهرام is the subject (placed after the verb for emphasis). کای شاه هند is a vocative phrase: کای 'O' addressing the addressee, شاه 'king', هند 'India' — 'O king of India.'
segments.s632-g14: فرستادگان را is the direct object with the definite/object marker را ('the envoys'). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)', and ناپسند is an adjectival predicate 'unsuitable/unpleasant/unacceptable'. Together they form the prohibition 'do not make the envoys displeasing/unwelcome' (i.e. 'do not offend/displease the envoys').
segments.s632-g15: نه ... نه is a correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. از تخمه یزدگردم literally 'from the seed/lineage of Yazdegerd' uses از + تخمه 'of/from the offspring' plus the name یزدگرد (here with enclitic -م indicating 'I am of'). The phrase denies descent from Yazdegerd's family.
segments.s632-g16: برادرش خوانیم is a clause 'we call/name his brother' (خوانیم = we call/say; here the verb of naming/being called). باشد گناه is a fixed expression 'it would be a sin' (باشد = 'it would be/is' in subordinate/potential sense; گناه = 'sin/blame'). Together the segment denies making the brother king or calling him such, adding that doing so would be sinful.
segments.s632-g33: تو گویی is a 2nd-person present/optative expression meaning 'you say' or 'you would say' (here more like 'you would say'). مر is an enclitic/old form meaning 'to me' (dative); او را is the topical/accusative-marked pronoun 'him' (را marks the object). نکوتر بود is the comparative/adjective نکو + -تر with بود (past/copular) — 'was better/kinder.' Word order is topical/emphatic: dative (مر) precedes the object (او را) and the copula follows the adjective, a common classical/poetic pattern.
segments.s632-g34: This clause addresses 'you' again (تو) and uses گوی here as a deverbal/nominal use 'the one who says' or 'speaker' (poetic). آن introduces or points back ('that one' / 'that'), با وی = 'with him', and که در خور بود is a relative/subordinate clause: که introduces 'which/that', در خور is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'befitting / appropriate' (خور here is part of that compound), and بود is the copula in past. Overall: 'you are that speaker with him which was fitting.'
segments.s632-g35: بگویش is the imperative/command بگو + enclitic ش = 'tell him'. بران رو is an archaic two-word directive meaning 'go thither/that way' (بران = 'to/at that place' + رو = 'go/toward'); together they form the object/direction of the command. که باشد صواب is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/so that' or 'where'), with باشد in the subjunctive/optative sense 'it be' (here 'it is proper') and صواب 'proper/right'.
segments.s632-g36: که here functions as a causal/explanatory 'for/because'. پیش شه هند = 'in the presence of / before the king of Hind' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', شه = 'king', هند = 'India/Indians'). بفزودی is an adverb 'soon/quickly'. آب 'water' is the noun; in poetry the verb that governs it may be omitted or deferred, so this phrase indicates that 'water will soon be (there / forthcoming) before the Indian king.'
segments.s632-g37: کنون‌گر is an archaic conditional particle meaning 'if now' or 'if at present' (introducing a conditional). بباشی is the present/subjunctive form of بود used here in the sense 'you be' / 'you remain' (literally 'if you are'); به نزدیک اوی = 'near him' (به = 'to/at', نزدیک = 'near', اوی = old/pronounced form of 'him' or 'his') — the whole segment is the protasis 'if you are now near him.'
segments.s632-g38: نگه‌داری here functions as a verbal noun or exhortation 'keep/guard/attend to' (in classical usage it can carry an imperative sense). آن 'that' modifies رای 'counsel/plan' (رای = counsel/plan), باریک 'delicate/subtle' qualifies رای, and اوی 'his' marks possession. The sequence says: 'keep safe that subtle counsel of his.'
segments.s632-g39: هرانجا که is a fixed locative-subordinate 'wherever/whenever'. خوشتر is the comparative of خوش 'more pleasing/better'. ولایت is 'governorship/province/authority' (subject of the clause). تراست is an archaic copular/possessive form meaning 'was yours' or 'belonged to you' (ت + راست structure, classical idiom). So: 'wherever the governorship was more pleasing to you...'.
segments.s632-g40: This is a coordinated list of abstract nouns: سپهداری 'generalship/command', و 'and', باژ 'cry/noise' (here meaning public renown/fame in context), و 'and', ملکت 'realm/sovereignty'. تراست repeats the copular/possessive 'was yours', applying to the whole list — 'command, fame, and kingdom were yours.' The conjunctions simply join the items and the final تراست ties them to the preceding clause.
segments.s632-g17: از ایران is a prepositional phrase placed at the front, meaning 'from Iran'. یکی مرد is an indefinite phrase 'a certain / a single man' (poetic/archaic usage) and بیگانه‌ام is a nominal predicate with the 1st-person copula -ام attached to the adjective 'بیگانه' = 'I am a stranger'. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (place of origin first).
segments.s632-g18: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. دانش پژوهم functions as a compound idea 'one who pursues/has learning' (i.e. learning/knowledge-seeking) and is negated; فرزانه‌ام is again a nominal predicate with -ام 'I am (not) wise'. The clauses are parallel: the first denies learned inquiry/knowledge, the second denies wisdom.
segments.s632-g19: مرا بازگردان is an imperative 'return/send me back' where م + را together mark the accusative object 'me' (here listed separately). که introduces the following clause giving the reason ('for' / 'because'). دورست راه is an inverted adjective-noun order ('the road is distant/long')—poetic inversion placing the adjective before the noun.
segments.s632-g20: نباید که is a formula meaning 'it must not / it should not be that...' followed by یابد (3rd-person present/subjunctive of یافتن) 'find/overcome'. The clause places the verb یابد before its object مرا (here split) and the subject خشم شاه after, a poetic inversion meaning 'the king's anger must not find me'.
segments.s632-g21: بدو گفت شنگل is an inverted order common in poetry: dative/oblique particle 'بدو' ('to him') comes before the verb گفت ('said') and the speaker's name شنگل follows the verb ('Shangal said to him'). که تندی مکن is a subordinate imperative: که gives the content/reason and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (be)' attached to تندی 'haste'.
segments.s632-g22: که introduces the reason ('for' / 'because'). با تو هنوزست ما را سخن has poetic word order: 'هنوزست' ('is still') functions as the verb, followed by 'ما را سخن' ('words of ours' / 'we still have words') with را marking the direct object/possession; literally, 'with you there still is our speech' = 'we still have things to say to you.'
segments.s632-g23: نبایدت is نباید plus the 2nd-person clitic -ت, yielding 'it should not be for you' i.e. 'you should not'. کردن is the light verb 'to do/make' used here in a causative/construct with the following infinitive phrase; به رفتن ('to going') marks the action (literally 'to the going'), and شتاب is the noun 'haste'. Together they mean 'you should not make haste in going' = 'do not hurry to leave.'
segments.s632-g24: که introduces the explanatory clause ('for/because'). رفتن is the verbal noun 'leaving'; به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'soon/quickly' where به marks the adverbial use; نباشد is the negated subjunctive/present of 'to be' meaning 'would not be' or 'is not' and appears before the predicate صواب—poetic order 'نباشد صواب' meaning 'would not be right.'
segments.s632-g81: همان به که is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'it is best/it is fitting that'; که introduces the subordinate clause. من باز گردم is a first‑person clause where باز 'again' precedes the verb; گردم is an old/classical present/subjunctive/optative form ('that I return' / 'may I return') rather than a simple past. بدر here functions as a place or name (a destination) placed after the verb.
segments.s632-g82: ببیند is a third‑person present/subjunctive/optative form (ب‌ + بیند) meaning 'may he see' or 'that he see'. مرا is split in the manuscript into م + را: م is the object pronoun 'me' and را is the definite/object marker attached after it; together مرا = 'me (as direct object)'. شاه پیروزگر is the subject with a postpositive epithet (پیروزگر 'victorious' modifying شاه). Word order places verb before subject and the object pronoun before the object marker.
segments.s632-g83: گر is the conditional 'if'. از نام پرسیم literally 'if (we/one) ask about the name': از + noun ('about/concerning'), and پرسیم here is the classical present/subjunctive/optative form used in hypothetical clauses ('we/one ask' or 'should one ask'). برزوی نام places the proper name 'Barzū' next to 'name' (apposition: 'the name Barzū').
segments.s632-g84: چنین خواندم is a past narration: خواندم ('I called/named/said') with چنین 'thus/so' — 'I called/said thus.' خواندم here means 'I called (by name) / I named / I said.' The list شاه و هم باب و مام is an enumeration of the forms of address used (شاه 'king', هم 'also', باب and مام are forms of address — dialectal/archaic 'father'/'mother' in this context); و is the coordination 'and'.
segments.s632-g85: همه is a vocative/imperative address 'all (of you)'. پاسخ من is the direct object 'my answer/reply' (پاسخ + possessive من). بشنگل is a proper name or address (the intended recipient); رسان is either the imperative or present form of رساندن 'deliver/carry' (here an instruction: 'deliver/bring'). The verb follows the object phrase, which is common in epic/formal Persian imperatives and commands.
segments.s632-g86: که introduces the content of the message: 'that I stayed late...' من دیر ماندم is the autobiographical clause where دیر 'late/for a long time' modifies ماندم (past 'I remained/stayed'). به شهر کسان is a prepositional phrase ('in/at the town among people') with به marking location; کسان 'people' clarifies in whose town/among whom he stayed.
segments.s632-g87: چو here = 'when'. دستور بشنید is a past narrative clause: دستور 'the order/command' is the object and بشنید is the past 'he heard' (classical past). پاسخ ببرد is the following action: ببرد is the third‑person form meaning 'he took/carried' (in narrative sequence; ببرد often expresses the subsequent action 'he carried off / he took'). Together they describe 'when he heard the command, he took the reply.'
segments.s632-g88: شنیده is the perfect/participial form 'having heard' (or 'after he had heard'), followed by سخن 'the words/speech' as object. پیش او means 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + pronoun او). برشمرد is a simple past 'he recounted/enumerated'. The clause therefore reads 'having heard the words, he recounted them before him.'
segments.s632-g25: بر ما is a prepositional phrase meaning literally 'upon us' but here idiomatically 'with us' (object of the imperative). بباش is an imperative-form (be/stay); word-order is verb after prepositional phrase (typical in Persian imperatives). و links the two imperatives. دل آرام گیر is another imperative clause: دل (direct object) + آرام (adjective) + گیر (imperative of گرفتن 'take' used idiomatically = 'make/keep calm').
segments.s632-g26: چو introduces a conditional 'if/when'. پخته is a participial/adjectival word 'cooked/ripe/ready'. نخواهی is second-person present negative 'you will not/won't want' (used in the protasis). می‌خام is first-person present 'I want' (here rhetorical) and گیر is again an imperative 'take/seize' — together the clause reads literally 'if you will not when (it is) ripe, take (it) while I want' but idiomatically 'if you won't accept it when fully ready, take it now.'
segments.s632-g27: پس‌انگاه is an adverb 'afterwards/then'. دستور را is the definite object (را marks دستور) — here 'دستور' refers to the command/commanders/officials called; پیش خواند is a verb phrase 'called forward/ summoned' with پیش 'before/forward' and خواند (past) 'called/summoned'. Word order is adverb → object → verb, common in narrative past.
segments.s632-g28: ز here is the archaic/prepositional 'from' (or 'by') introducing the source/agent; بهرام is the proper name. با او 'with him' indicates the person addressed/attending. سخن چند راند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سخن 'words/speech' + چند 'a few/several' + راند (past of راندن) used here to mean 'uttered/said a few words' — the verb rending carries 'uttered/drove out' sense in poetic register.
segments.s632-g29: گر is a conditional 'if' (also used like 'though' in poetry). این مرد is 'this man/person'. بهرام را uses را as a topical/definite marker ('Bahram' as the person in question). خویش نیست is a predicative construction: خویش (kin/own) + possessive suffix ش = 'his own' and نیست 'is not' — 'is not his own' or 'is not truly Bahram's.' Word order is conditional → subject → object/topic → predicate.
segments.s632-g30: گر begins the second conditional clause. از here functions as 'of'/'from' and introduces the partitive sense. پهلوان نام او بیش نیست is a verbless nominal clause: پهلوان نام 'hero-name' (i.e. 'heroism' or 'reputation as a champion') + او 'his' + بیش نیست lit. 'is not more' — idiomatically 'there is nothing more than his name' or 'only his name remains' (i.e. he has fame in name only).
segments.s632-g31: چو here = 'when/as'. گویی is a noun/gerund from گفت‌وگو/گویش meaning 'speech/words' (in poetic diction it can mean 'if he says' or 'if by his words'); دهد is the present/past-form 'gives/causes' (here 'utters/causes'), او = 'he'. تن‌اندر is a compound adverbial 'in the body' / 'bodily, outwardly' and فریب is the noun 'deception' — together 'when by his speech he leads the body into deception' or 'when he, by his words, deceives in person.' The clause is conditional/temporal.
segments.s632-g32: گر here is conditional 'if'. از گفت من = 'from/my saying' (از introducing the source: 'by what I say' or 'from my words'). در دل آرد is a common Persian structure: در دل 'into the heart' + آرد (present/past-subjunctive form of آوردن 'bring/put') meaning 'he brings/puts into his heart'. نهیب is a noun 'rebuke/stern reproof' — the whole clause: 'if my words put a rebuke into his heart.'
segments.s632-g49: چو ... باشی introduces a conditional/subordinate clause: چو = 'if/when' and گفته باشی is a perfect/subjunctive construction meaning 'you have said' (گفته + باشی as auxiliary). به پرسش ز نام is a prepositional phrase 'in/when asking about the name' where به marks the relation to 'پرسش' and ز (= 'concerning/of') links پرسش to نام.
segments.s632-g50: که introduces the result/purpose clause ('so that / that'); از نام = 'from/by virtue of the name' (ز here = 'from/of'), گردد is the present/subjunctive form of گردیدن 'become' (here = 'may become / will become'), and دلم is دل + enclitic م = 'my heart'; شادکام = 'glad/fortunate'.
segments.s632-g51: مگر here introduces a conditional/wishful clause (classical usage approximating 'if' / 'if only'); رام گردد = 'becomes tame' (گردد as present/subjunctive of شدن/گشتن). بدین مرز ما = 'in/at this frontier of ours' (ب = preposition 'to/at', مرز = 'frontier', ما = 'our').
segments.s632-g52: فزون گردد is 'will/increase/ grow' (فزون = 'more/increasing', گردد = 'become/happen'); از فر او = 'by/from his far' (فر = royal glory/favour, او = 'his'); ارز ما = 'our worth/value' (word order places the possessed noun at the end).
segments.s632-g53: ورا = 'to him' (object/recipient); زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly'; سالار لشکر is a compound noun 'commander of the army' (سالار = commander/title before لشکر); کنیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us make / we will make'.
segments.s632-g54: بدین مرز = 'to/at this frontier' (ب + this), با ارز ما = 'with our wealth' (با = 'with', ارز ما = 'our worth/wealth'), and سر کنیم uses سر (literally 'head') with کردن to mean 'to set/appoint (as head)'; کنيم again is the first‑person plural verb 'let us make/appoint'.
segments.s632-g55: بیامد is simple past 'came/arrived'; جهاندیده is an epithet 'world‑wise / experienced' functioning as the subject; دستور شاه = 'the king's order' (دستور governed by شاه as genitive). The construction implies 'the experienced one came with the king's instruction'.
segments.s632-g56: بگفت = 'he said / told' (past), این = 'this' as the content of the speech, به + بهرام marks the indirect object 'to Bahram', و = 'and', بنمود = 'showed', راه = 'the way/path' (بنمود راه = 'showed the way'). Note the classical verbal prefix ب- on verbs (بگفت, بنمود).

sentences.s632-s21: Wherever there is an ever-spring place, the spring breeze comes from the stream.
sentences.s632-s22: There are jewels and dinars and treasure (in the purse); when there is money, the heart is not in sorrow.
sentences.s632-s23: A royal musician who, out of love for you, laughs whenever he sees your face.
sentences.s632-s24: In a year the tree bears fruit twice; the fortunate one does not pass beyond Kannauj.
sentences.s632-s29: Then he asked Bahram his name, for without a name a reply would not be complete.
sentences.s632-s30: When Bahram heard the color/appearance of his steed, his looks changed, for he wondered how he should give his answer.
sentences.s632-s31: At last he said, 'O eloquent man, do not make my face pale in two realms.'
sentences.s632-s32: I will not beg treasure from the King of Iran; if I am but a nothing, let me endure hardship.
sentences.s632-s37: Where are those great scions of Khosrow — the sovereigns Kay Khosrow and Kay Qobad?
sentences.s632-s38: And what of that other one you know — Bahram, the sovereign, victorious and autocratic?
sentences.s632-s39: If I disobey his command, the world will raise a man against me.
sentences.s632-s40: No homeland of Hindustan will remain; the soil of the sorcerers will be carried into Iran.
sentences.s632-s45: On hearing the reply the king's face took on an odd/strange look, and he said, “If he should remain far from the road…”
sentences.s632-s46: I will now devise a remedy so that a day may come that will make this man's army glorious.
sentences.s632-s33: May the ornament of our faith be none other than this: the same course of our way and custom.
sentences.s632-s34: Whoever turns his head away from his king, when he departs will lose his way.
sentences.s632-s35: He who had wisdom did not seek increase; bad and good alike still pass over us.
sentences.s632-s36: Where is Fereydun, lord of the world, who was the true support of the age?
sentences.s632-s1: Because of Bahram, Shangal became uneasy, thinking that this farr and this exaltation and (these) arrows and bows...
sentences.s632-s2: This messenger was left with none — neither Indian, nor Turk, nor noble.
sentences.s632-s3: If by nature he is kingly, or if he is the elder, I call him my brother; he too is among the diners/at the table.
sentences.s632-s4: The king laughed and said to Bahram: 'O cultured one, present the gem before the court.'
sentences.s632-s5: Your brother, O king, is surely (so) with these gifts, strength, bow and arrows.
sentences.s632-s6: for he possesses the royal glory of the Kians and the strength of a lion; you would not be so except for the renown of a brave man.
sentences.s632-s7: To him Bahram said, 'O king of India, do not make the envoys disliked.'
sentences.s632-s8: I am neither of Yazdegerd's seed nor shall we call his brother a king — that would be a sin.
sentences.s632-s17: You would say that he was kinder to me; you are the one who spoke with him as was fitting.
sentences.s632-s18: Tell him to go there where it will be proper, for before the king of Hind the water will soon (be).
sentences.s632-s19: If you are now near him, keep safe that subtle counsel of his.
sentences.s632-s20: Wherever the governorship pleased you more, command, renown, and sovereignty were yours.
sentences.s632-s9: I am a lone stranger from Iran; I have neither learning nor wisdom.
sentences.s632-s10: Send me back, for the road is long; the king's wrath must not find me.
sentences.s632-s11: Shangal said to him, 'Do not be hasty, for we still have words to speak with you.'
sentences.s632-s12: You should not hurry to depart, for leaving quickly would not be right.
sentences.s632-s41: It is best that I return again to Badr; may the victorious king see me.
sentences.s632-s42: If one asks about the name, I answered 'Barzū' — I called him thus: 'king' and also 'bab' and 'mam'.
sentences.s632-s43: All of you, carry my reply to Bashangol; tell him that I stayed long in the town among people.
sentences.s632-s44: When he heard the order, he took the reply; having heard the words, he recounted them before him.
sentences.s632-s13: Stay with us and put your heart at rest; if you will not take it once it is fully ripe, seize it now.
sentences.s632-s14: Then he called the officers forward; he said a few words to Bahram.
sentences.s632-s15: If this man is not truly Bahram's, if of the champion only his name remains.
sentences.s632-s16: When by his speech he beguiles the body, if my words put a rebuke into his heart.
sentences.s632-s25: If, when asking about the name, you have said this, then my heart will be made glad because of the name.
sentences.s632-s26: If he is tamed in this frontier, our worth will increase through his farr.
sentences.s632-s27: Let us soon make him commander of the army; let us set him up at this frontier with our wealth as chief.
sentences.s632-s28: The experienced envoy arrived with the king's order; he told this to Bahram and showed the way.
